does: This paper introduces MaskForge, a black-box adaptive attack method specifically designed to jailbreak diffusion-based large language models (dLLMs) that generate text through iterative denoising of masked sequences. The core contribution exploits the structural vulnerability unique to dLLMs: their bidirectional context processing and reliance on mask tokens as native inputs enable harmful content generation through infilling and beyond monitored prefixes. MaskForge operates as an optimized search procedure over a growing library of structural attack patterns, abstracting successful jailbreaks into reusable schemas and selecting goal-compatible patterns using a UCB bandit algorithm. When the pattern library fails, the method invokes a scorer-guided fallback mechanism. Successfully discovered attacks are distilled back into the library, allowing accumulated attack experience to transfer across different harmful objectives and improve subsequent attempts.
connects: This work builds on recent research into dLLM safety vulnerabilities, which recognized that bidirectional denoising creates fundamentally different attack surfaces than autoregressive LLMs. It extends prior jailbreaking research by moving beyond existing approaches that either ignore the infill capability or apply uniform mask-bearing templates with minimal structural adaptation. The paper connects to the broader literature on adversarial attacks against language models, including both gradient-based and black-box red-teaming methods, while adapting bandit optimization and pattern-based search techniques from the adversarial ML community. By framing jailbreaking as pattern discovery and library optimization, it builds on meta-learning and experience-accumulation approaches in adversarial settings.
enables: MaskForge establishes a new paradigm for studying dLLM robustness by demonstrating systematic, transferable attack patterns against masked-generation architectures. This work enables future research into defensive mechanisms specifically tailored to bidirectional denoising models, such as improved masking strategies or output filtering tuned to infill vulnerabilities. The pattern library and accumulated experience framework suggests directions for building robust dLLMs with attack-resistant architectural designs and training procedures. The black-box adaptive attack approach could inform the development of more efficient red-teaming pipelines and evaluation benchmarks for dLLMs. Additionally, the insights about structural vulnerabilities in masked generation may guide the design of safer alternatives to dLLMs or hybrid approaches that mitigate infill-based jailbreaks while preserving generation quality.
